title: Zip-line supervisor could not recall doing checks before father's fatal fall
author: Jordan Bissell
fullText: A supervisor working when Adelaide father Dean Sanderson fell to his death off a zip-line in the Daintree could not recall conducting equipment checks earlier that day, a coronial inquest has heard.Aston Wenham, a three-time employee of Jungle Surfing Canopy Tours, told the inquest today he was working on the zip-line course on October 22, 2019, the day 50-year-old Mr Sanderson fell to his death and his wife Shannon Sanderson was injured. Mr Wenham, who was belaying the pair as they zip-lined between two platforms, recounted the moment he felt the rope go slack and saw the couple drop to the ground."I did look around," he said. "I could see that something wasn't right, that something had broken … I didn't know exactly what had happened."I said on the radio that a cable broke, but afterwards I realised that I didn't know what happened."Jungle Surfing Canopy Tours ceased operations weeks after Mr Sanderson's death. (ABC News: Marian Faa)He said he spoke to another staff member on the radio, but turned the radio down shortly afterwards because it was distressing to other tourists.  Mr Wenham said he did not recall conducting checks on the equipment on the day of Mr Sanderson's death, but said as a supervisor at the company, he was involved in daily "visual and touch" checks of the equipment, and said "monthly" more "formal" checks were conducted as well.He did not recall whether engineers or an external company had ever been brought in to conduct inspections during the course of his employment.When questioned about staff training at the company, he told the inquest some of the training at Jungle Surfing Canopy Tours was "self-directed" and other training was delivered by external training agencies. Mr Wenham said he acquired certificates for working at heights and conducting rescues at heights whilst working at the Cape Tribulation company.Tour operations manager not aware of WH&S regulations Former Jungle Surfing Canopy Tours tour operations manager James Dalzell gave evidence at the inquest via video. He said he had been in that position at the time of Mr Sanderson's death and was responsible for training staff on how to conduct checks on the zip-lines. The inquest heard before Mr Sanderson's death new rope grips had been installed on the course by staff. Dean Sanderson died in a zip-line accident in the Daintree Rainforest in 2019.  (Supplied)Mr Dalzell was shown a warning on an invoice for wire rope grips, which read "not for lifting".He said if he had seen that warning at the time, he might have had cause for concern over their use.He admitted he was not aware of a series of Workplace Health and Safety regulations the site appeared not to have complied with, including the need for annual checks by an engineer.Mr Dalzell did not recall the course being inspected in its entirety by an engineer before customers were allowed to use it.Jungle Surfing Canopy Tours ceased operations weeks after Mr Sanderson's death, but Mr Dalzell continued working on-site until 2020, as it was prepared for sale.The inquest is expected to continue until Thursday, before resuming for a fifth day of evidence on March 10.
summary: The supervisor who was belaying Dean and Shannon Sanderson as they zip-lined between two platforms, recounted the moment he felt the rope go slack and saw the couple drop to the ground.
